exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat/chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and your arms can reach the handles with a slight elbow bend at the start. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat/chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and your arms can reach the handles with a slight elbow bend at the start.
Load the desired plates onto the machine and sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the pad, and eyes forward.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, shoulders relaxed; start with the arms extended and the weight stacked (controlled start).
Exhale and pull the handles straight back by driving your elbows toward your ribs, leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end range (handle close to torso) and hold the contraction for 1 second; avoid shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position with a controlled eccentric, allowing the arms to fully extend while maintaining chest contact with the pad.
Repeat for the prescribed reps; avoid leaning back or using torso momentum and keep wrist/forearm tension under control for consistent technique.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever row performed with chest support to isolate the back; you pull the handles toward the torso to drive scapular retraction and elbow extension. Emphasizes horizontal pulling to develop the lats and upper back while minimizing torso momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is around 30–45 degrees (or nearly parallel to the floor) with a soft bend in the knees; keep the spine neutral and neck aligned. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is around 30–45 degrees (or nearly parallel to the floor) with a soft bend in the knees; keep the spine neutral and neck aligned.
Let the dumbbells hang under your shoulders with elbows slightly bent (not locked). Brace your core to prevent swinging.

Exhale and raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, leading with the elbows and keeping the slight elbow bend, until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor or in line with the shoulders.
Pause and squeeze the rear delts and upper back at the top for 1 second, then inhale and lower the weights slowly under control back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding momentum—keep the movement slow and controlled and maintain the hip hinge throughout.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell raise that isolates the rear (posterior) deltoids by lifting the arms out to the sides while the torso is hinged forward. Performed with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum and emphasize the rear delts and upper back.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Lever Front Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine weight and set the seat height so your feet are flat on the floor and the thigh pads rest snugly on your thighs to prevent lifting. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the machine weight and set the seat height so your feet are flat on the floor and the thigh pads rest snugly on your thighs to prevent lifting.
Sit upright on the seat (seated), reach up and grasp the front/wide handles with an overhand (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder width; arms should start almost fully extended.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back (scapular depression/retraction) before initiating the pull to engage the lats.
Exhale and pull the handles straight down toward the upper chest/front, leading with your elbows and keeping your torso mostly upright (allow a very slight lean back but avoid excessive swinging).
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the bottom of the movement, keeping the wrists neutral and elbows pointing down/back, then pause briefly.
Inhale and slowly control the weight as you extend your arms back up to the start position, allowing the shoulder blades to fully stretch without shrugging the shoulders.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping motion smooth and avoiding momentum or pulling with the neck; lower weight if you cannot maintain full scapular control.
exercises.description: Seated lever front pulldown using a wide overhead handle to pull resistance down in front of the chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus region. Performed seated with thigh pads to stabilize the body while pulling the handles to the upper chest.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Row
exercises.notes: Reach forward and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, keeping your shoulders down and away from your ears. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the machine with your chest firmly against the pad, feet flat on the footrests and knees slightly bent; adopt a tall, neutral spine.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, keeping your shoulders down and away from your ears.
Brace your core and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together (scapular retraction) and driving your elbows straight back.
Continue pulling until the handles approach your lower ribs/abdomen while squeezing the lats and upper back; avoid shrugging or excessive elbow flare.
Slowly extend your arms back to the start while allowing the shoulder blades to protract, controlling the weight on the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, keeping motion smooth and avoiding torso rocking, momentum, or rounding of the lower back.
exercises.description: Seated lever row on a chest-supported machine that pulls a handle toward the torso, primarily targeting the lats and middle back while also engaging the arms and rear shoulders.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Fly
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight and grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip, arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows (no locked joints). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the machine with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor; adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at mid-chest level.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip, arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows (no locked joints).
Brace your core and keep your chest up and shoulders down and back throughout the movement (seated, back supported).
Exhale and pull the handles together in a smooth arc in front of your chest, leading with the elbows and focusing on squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly at full contraction while maintaining the slight elbow bend and scapula position (do not let shoulders shrug).
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position under control, allowing a stretch in the chest but avoiding excessive shoulder extension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining consistent tempo and full contact with the back pad.
Common form cues: keep elbows slightly bent, avoid using momentum or leaning forward, and do not let wrists or forearms drive the movement—lead with the chest.
exercises.description: Seated lever fly performed on a chest fly machine to isolate and contract the pectorals by bringing the arms together in front of the torso. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and a controlled arc to emphasize chest contraction.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

exercises.name: Lever Chest Press (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Set up: place the desired plates on the machine pegs and lock any safety pins. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set up: place the desired plates on the machine pegs and lock any safety pins. Adjust the seat height so the handles sit level with the mid-chest (sternal area) when seated.
Sit upright with your back flat against the pad, feet flat on the floor and shoulder-width apart. Grip the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip, wrists straight.
Retract your shoulder blades slightly and keep your chest proud to stabilize the torso throughout the movement.
Start with your elbows bent and hands at chest level; this is the starting position.
Exhale and press the handles forward and slightly together by extending the elbows until your arms are nearly straight. Do not fully lock out the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals while maintaining tight core and stable shoulders.
Inhale and slowly control the levers back to the starting position, lowering until your elbows reach about chest level or a comfortable stretch is felt in the pectorals.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding rapid bouncing or excessive torso movement.
When finished, return the levers to the safe/locked position before releasing the handles and unloading plates if needed.
exercises.description: A seated plate-loaded lever chest press that targets the pectorals by pressing fixed lever arms forward from chest level; the anterior delts and triceps assist. The machine provides a guided pressing path for controlled concentric and eccentric phases.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat/chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and your arms can reach the handles with a slight elbow bend at the start. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat/chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and your arms can reach the handles with a slight elbow bend at the start.
Load the desired plates onto the machine and sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the pad, and eyes forward.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, shoulders relaxed; start with the arms extended and the weight stacked (controlled start).
Exhale and pull the handles straight back by driving your elbows toward your ribs, leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end range (handle close to torso) and hold the contraction for 1 second; avoid shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position with a controlled eccentric, allowing the arms to fully extend while maintaining chest contact with the pad.
Repeat for the prescribed reps; avoid leaning back or using torso momentum and keep wrist/forearm tension under control for consistent technique.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever row performed with chest support to isolate the back; you pull the handles toward the torso to drive scapular retraction and elbow extension. Emphasizes horizontal pulling to develop the lats and upper back while minimizing torso momentum.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat/chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and your arms can reach the handles with a slight elbow bend at the start. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat/chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and your arms can reach the handles with a slight elbow bend at the start.
Load the desired plates onto the machine and sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the pad, and eyes forward.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, shoulders relaxed; start with the arms extended and the weight stacked (controlled start).
Exhale and pull the handles straight back by driving your elbows toward your ribs, leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end range (handle close to torso) and hold the contraction for 1 second; avoid shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position with a controlled eccentric, allowing the arms to fully extend while maintaining chest contact with the pad.
Repeat for the prescribed reps; avoid leaning back or using torso momentum and keep wrist/forearm tension under control for consistent technique.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever row performed with chest support to isolate the back; you pull the handles toward the torso to drive scapular retraction and elbow extension. Emphasizes horizontal pulling to develop the lats and upper back while minimizing torso momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright (feet about hip-width), hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip at your sides, knees softly bent and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand upright (feet about hip-width), hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip at your sides, knees softly bent and core braced.
Keep your arms straight (no shrugging with the elbows) and maintain a neutral spine and head position throughout.
Exhale and lift (shrug) both shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as you can, focusing on contracting the top of the traps.
Pause and squeeze at the top for 1–2 seconds, avoiding rolling or rotating the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the starting position with control until arms are fully relaxed at your sides.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping movement vertical and controlled; increase weight only if you can maintain form.
exercises.description: Standing exercise using dumbbells to raise the shoulders toward the ears, primarily working the trapezius muscles to develop upper-back and neck strength. Performed with a short vertical shrugging motion while keeping the arms straight.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Curl to Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides in a neutral grip (palms facing inward). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides in a neutral grip (palms facing inward).

Initiate a controlled dumbbell curl by rotating the palms to face upward and bending the elbows to bring the dumbbells up to shoulder level; keep elbows close to your sides and avoid swinging the torso.

At the top of the curl with dumbbells near shoulder height, rotate palms so they face forward and press the weights overhead by extending the elbows and driving through the shoulders until arms are fully extended.
Pause briefly at full extension with the ribs down and core engaged; avoid overarching the lower back.
Lower the dumbbells back down in a controlled motion to shoulder height, then reverse the curl movement to return the weights to your sides in a controlled manner.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the press and inhaling as you lower; maintain a steady tempo and avoid using momentum from the hips or legs.
exercises.description: A standing combination exercise that starts with a dumbbell curl and transitions into an overhead press, targeting the shoulder muscles while also working the arms. Perform the curl to bring weights to shoulder level, then press the dumbbells overhead and control the descent back to the start.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Single Arm Front Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent at ~90°, and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent at ~90°, and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip (palm facing your thigh or down). Place the non-working hand on the opposite thigh for support.
Start with the working arm hanging down in front of your hip, elbow slightly bent and shoulder relaxed.

Exhale and raise the dumbbell straight forward in a controlled arc until your arm is parallel to the floor (about shoulder height). Keep a slight bend in the elbow and lead with the hand, not the elbow.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the torso still — avoid leaning back or using hip drive.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position under control.
Perform the desired reps on one side, then switch arms. Maintain steady tempo and avoid swinging or using momentum.
exercises.description: A seated single-arm front raise isolates the front (anterior) deltoid by lifting a dumbbell straight in front of the body to shoulder height while seated, minimizing momentum. It strengthens shoulder flexion and improves anterior shoulder definition and stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Palm Rotational Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Keep a neutral spine and neck (eyes toward the floor a few feet in front of you). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand. Slightly bend the knees and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (bent-over position).
Keep a neutral spine and neck (eyes toward the floor a few feet in front of you). Let the dumbbells hang at arm's length beneath your shoulders with a pronated or neutral grip.
Brace your core and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction).

Pull the dumbbells upward toward your lower ribs/waist by driving the elbows up and back. As you ascend, rotate your wrists so the palms turn (for example from pronation/neutral toward supination) at the top of the row.
Squeeze the upper back and rear delts at the top for a brief pause, keeping elbows close to the body and avoiding excessive torso rotation or momentum.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position while rotating the palms back to the initial orientation, maintaining control throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps. Maintain the hip hinge, slight knee bend, and neutral spine for the entire set.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell row variation where the wrists/palms rotate during the pull to increase scapular retraction and arm involvement. Performed in a hip-hinged position, it emphasizes the upper back while also engaging the lats, rear delts and arms.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Single Arm Alternate Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the hips. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the hips.

Hold a dumbbell in one hand with a neutral grip (thumbs slightly up) and extend that arm straight over the chest; keep the opposite arm either resting lightly or holding a dumbbell vertical for stability.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder joint and maintain a slight bend in the working elbow throughout the movement.

Inhale and slowly lower the working arm out to the side in a wide arc, leading from the shoulder until you feel a moderate stretch in the pectorals (stop before the elbow drops below bench level or shoulder discomfort occurs).
Pause briefly at the bottom, then exhale and drive the arm back up along the same arc, squeezing the pectoral muscle to bring the dumbbell back over the midline of the chest.
At the top, control the weight and briefly pause, then switch sides and perform the same number of reps with the other arm, alternating single-arm reps for the set.
Keep movements controlled (no bouncing), maintain the slight elbow bend, and avoid shrugging the working shoulder; reduce weight if you cannot keep form.
exercises.description: A single-arm, alternating dumbbell fly performed lying supine on a flat bench that isolates the chest by moving one arm at a time in a wide arc while the other arm remains stable. Keeps a slight bend in the elbow and emphasizes a slow controlled stretch and squeeze of the pectorals.

exercises.name: Lever Bent over Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Set the lever machine or plate-loaded bar to the desired weight and stand with feet hip-width under the bar/platform. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the lever machine or plate-loaded bar to the desired weight and stand with feet hip-width under the bar/platform.
Hinge at the hips with a soft bend in the knees until your torso is approximately 30–45 degrees from vertical (bent-over position); keep the spine neutral and neck in line with the torso.
Grasp the bar with an overhand (or chosen) grip about shoulder-width apart and let your arms hang straight down; engage your core to stabilize the trunk.
Initiate the rep by pulling your elbows back and driving the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen, squeezing the shoulder blades together at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a braced torso, then slowly lower the bar back to the start under control until the arms are extended.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding torso rotation, excessive momentum, or rounding of the lower back throughout the set.
exercises.description: A bent-over lever row performed from a hip-hinged position to drive the loaded bar toward the torso, emphasizing the mid/upper back. It develops pulling strength and upper-back thickness while also engaging the arms and rear shoulders.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing position). Train to muscular failure.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Alternative Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine (on your back) on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing each other. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Lie supine (on your back) on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing each other.
Start with arms extended out to the sides at chest level with a slight bend in the elbows and shoulders relaxed; this is the starting position.
Brace your core and keep your shoulder blades stable on the bench throughout the movement.

Initiate the movement by horizontally adducting (bringing across) one arm in a wide arc toward the midline, squeezing the pectorals as the dumbbell moves to meet or come close to the other hand over the chest.
While the working arm moves across, slowly lower the opposite arm back out to the starting wide position in a controlled manner; alternate arms so one rises while the other lowers.
Keep the elbow angle slightly bent and fixed (do not fully straighten or excessively bend the elbow), and avoid shrugging the shoulders or using momentum.
At the top of the movement pause briefly and control the descent on the working arm back to the wide start position, maintaining tension in the pectorals.
Perform the desired number of alternating reps, using a weight that allows strict form and full control on each rep.
exercises.description: A single‑arm (alternating) dumbbell fly performed lying on a bench to isolate the chest; you perform a controlled arc with one arm while the other returns to the start position. Emphasizes horizontal adduction of the shoulder to work the pectorals while the delts and scapular stabilizers assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Front Raise (version 2)
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing position). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or overhand grip and the weights resting in front of your thighs.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement to protect the joint and avoid locking.
Exhale and raise both dumbbells straight in front of you to roughly shoulder height, leading with the hands and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause for a second at the top while maintaining upright posture and a tight core; do not lean backward or use hip drive.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping movement slow and controlled to minimize momentum and maximize delt activation.
If balance or form breaks down, reduce the weight or perform the raise one arm at a time.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell front raise variation where both dumbbells are lifted together in front of the body to work the anterior shoulder. Performed with a slight bend in the elbow and controlled tempo to isolate the delts and minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Lever Incline Hammer Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set the bench to an incline (about 30–45 degrees) and select an appropriate weight on the lever machine. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the bench to an incline (about 30–45 degrees) and select an appropriate weight on the lever machine.
Sit on the incline bench with your back firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor, and hips stable.
Reach up and grasp the neutral (hammer) handles with a thumb-around grip so palms face each other; hands should start near the upper chest/shoulder line.
Retract your shoulder blades slightly and brace your core to maintain a stable torso throughout the lift.
Exhale and press the handles upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly straight but not locked out.
Pause briefly at the top with tension on the pectorals, then inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping wrists neutral and avoiding excessive flaring of the elbows or arching of the lower back.
exercises.description: Seated incline chest press using a neutral (hammer) grip on a lever machine that emphasizes the upper chest. The movement presses the handles upward and slightly forward from the level of the upper chest to near full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Decline Fly
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet in the foot pads so you can remain stable while supine. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet in the foot pads so you can remain stable while supine.
Pick two light-to-moderate dumbbells and sit down; then lie back so your head is lower than your hips (decline position) and your feet are anchored.
Start with the dumbbells held directly above your lower chest, palms facing each other (neutral grip), arms almost straight but with a slight, fixed bend in the elbows.
Brace your core and keep your shoulder blades pulled slightly together to stabilize the shoulder girdle.

Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to your sides in a controlled wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend; aim to lower until you feel a stretch in the lower chest but do not let the elbows drop behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, maintaining tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior.
Exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc using your chest, bringing them to meet above your lower sternum; avoid using momentum or locking the elbows at the top.
Perform each rep with a controlled tempo (2–4 seconds descent, 1 second concentric) and keep the wrists neutral; reduce weight if you feel shoulder pain.
When finished, bring the dumbbells safely to your thighs and sit up slowly to avoid dizziness from the decline position.
exercises.description: Lying supine on a decline bench, the dumbbell decline fly isolates the lower portion of the pectorals by moving the arms in a wide arc with a fixed elbow angle. It stretches the chest on the descent and contracts the chest to bring the weights together over the sternum.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back.
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at your thighs.

Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back. Retract your shoulder blades and keep them contacting the bench.
Start with the dumbbells at mid-chest, elbows bent about 90 degrees and wrists neutral (dumbbell handles aligned with forearms).
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly straight but not hyperextended; keep wrists stacked over elbows.

Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position, maintaining control and stopping when the dumbbells reach chest level or your elbows form ~90 degrees. Keep elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from your torso to protect the shoulders.
Keep your core braced and feet firmly planted for stability throughout each rep; avoid excessive flaring of the ribs or letting the shoulders lift off the bench.
Use a spotter for heavy loads and choose a weight that allows controlled full-range reps with good form.
exercises.description: Flat dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a bench, pressing two dumbbells upward from chest level to develop the pectorals; the movement also recruits the anterior delts and triceps for stability and lockout.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with your arms fully extended; keep your head aligned with your spine.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows back and up, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs or hip area while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, maintaining tightness through the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with controlled motion until your arms are extended.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, keeping the torso fixed (avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation) and continue to breathe: exhale while pulling, inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A bent-over row performed with dumbbells where you hinge at the hips and pull the weights toward your torso, emphasizing the lats and upper back. The movement also engages the arms and posterior chain to stabilize the hinged position.

exercises.name: Pull-up (neutral grip)
exercises.notes: Stand under the pull-up handles and reach up to grasp the parallel (neutral) grips with palms facing each other, hands about shoulder-width apart.
instructions: Stand under the pull-up handles and reach up to grasp the parallel (neutral) grips with palms facing each other, hands about shoulder-width apart.
Hang fully with arms extended, shoulders relaxed but slightly depressed and scapulae engaged (avoid hanging completely passive); cross or bend your legs to reduce swinging if needed.
Brace your core and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades down and together, then drive your elbows down toward your ribs while pulling your chest up toward the handles.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the level of your hands or your chest reaches the handles, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive kipping.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged, then lower yourself slowly and under control back to the full-hang position with arms extended.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining smooth controlled reps and avoiding momentum or excessive body swing.
exercises.description: A neutral-grip pull-up performed hanging from parallel handles with palms facing each other; you pull the torso upward until the chin clears the hands, emphasizing vertical pulling with strong lat engagement. This variation increases biceps involvement and is slightly easier on the shoulders than a wide pronated grip.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Hanging Leg Tuck
exercises.notes: Grip a pull-up bar with hands about shoulder-width apart and hang with arms fully extended (dead hang).
instructions: Grip a pull-up bar with hands about shoulder-width apart and hang with arms fully extended (dead hang). Keep feet off the floor and legs relaxed at the start.
Depress and slightly retract the shoulders to engage the lats and create a stable hanging position; maintain a firm grip to engage the forearms.
Brace your core and inhale, then exhale as you flex at the hips and curl your knees upward toward your chest, driving your pelvis up to maximize abdominal contraction.
Pause briefly at the top with knees near the chest while keeping the movement controlled and shoulders stable (avoid shrugging or excessive swinging).
Inhale and slowly lower your legs back to the starting dead-hang position with control until arms are straight and abs remain engaged.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. To progress, perform the same motion with straighter legs (leg raises) or add a slight pause at the top.
exercises.description: From a dead hang on a pull-up bar, bring the knees up toward the chest by flexing the hips and curling the pelvis to strongly contract the abdominal wall. The movement is controlled and avoids excessive swinging to isolate the abs.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Pull-up
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate assistance (counterweight) on the assisted pull-up/dip machine and lower the knee pad to a comfortable height.
instructions: Select an appropriate assistance (counterweight) on the assisted pull-up/dip machine and lower the knee pad to a comfortable height.
Stand facing the bar and grasp it with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Mount the machine and kneel on the pad with knees supported and feet either crossed or tucked behind; allow the arms to fully extend so your body hangs straight.
Brace your core, keep your head neutral, and pull your shoulder blades down and back to initiate the movement.
Exhale and pull yourself upward by driving your elbows down and back, bringing your chin or upper chest toward the bar while squeezing the lats and upper back.
Pause briefly at the top with the scapulae retracted and avoid excessive shrugging of the delts.
Inhale and slowly lower yourself under control until your arms are fully extended and shoulders are not shrugged.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; reduce assistance (use a lighter counterweight) as you get stronger to increase difficulty.
exercises.description: Assisted pull-up on a counterweight machine where the user kneels on the support pad and pulls their body up to the bar; this exercise primarily targets the lats while also working the pulling muscles of the arms and upper back.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and push your butt back until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (slight knee bend), keeping the spine neutral and eyes looking a few feet ahead.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or pronated grip.
Hinge at the hips and push your butt back until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (slight knee bend), keeping the spine neutral and eyes looking a few feet ahead.
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with weights beneath you; brace your core to prevent rounding of the lower back.
Pull the dumbbells toward your lower ribcage or waist by driving your elbows up and back, squeezing the shoulder blades together at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a tight upper back, then lower the weights slowly under control until the arms are extended.
Repeat for the desired reps while keeping the torso still—avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation.
Breathe out as you row the weights up and inhale as you lower them; stop the set if you cannot maintain a neutral spine.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell row is a standing, hip-hinged pulling exercise that targets the muscles of the upper back while also engaging the lats and arms. It develops horizontal pulling strength and scapular retraction while the spine and forearms stabilize the position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and extend the arms straight above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement (do not lock the elbows).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, maintaining the slight elbow bend until you feel a stretch in the chest or your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep the shoulder blades stable on the bench (no excessive shrugging) and avoid letting the dumbbells travel behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then exhale and bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc, focusing on squeezing the pectorals until the weights meet above the sternum.
Perform each rep with controlled tempo and avoid bouncing or using momentum; select a weight that allows full control and a safe range of motion.
exercises.description: A supine resistance exercise performed with dumbbells that isolates the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from above the sternum to the sides, producing a deep stretch and concentric squeeze of the pectorals.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back. Retract your shoulder blades and keep them contacting the bench.
Start with the dumbbells at mid-chest, elbows bent about 90 degrees and wrists neutral (dumbbell handles aligned with forearms).

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with your arms fully extended; keep your head aligned with your spine.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows back and up, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs or hip area while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, maintaining tightness through the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with controlled motion until your arms are extended.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Alternate Upper Chest Raise
exercises.notes: Start standing upright with feet hip-width apart, a slight soft bend in the knees and a 10–20° forward lean from the hips (not rounding the spine).
instructions: Start standing upright with feet hip-width apart, a slight soft bend in the knees and a 10–20° forward lean from the hips (not rounding the spine). Hold a light dumbbell or handle in one hand with the arm hanging at your side and the palm facing your body.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted and shoulders down (no shrugging). Maintain a slight bend in the elbow throughout the movement.

Begin the rep by raising the working arm in a diagonal path across the front of your body and up toward the opposite shoulder/upper chest area (about 30–45° inward of straight front raise), rotating the palm slightly inward as you ascend.
Pause for 1 second at the top and actively squeeze the upper pectorals without letting the torso twist or the shoulder hike toward your ear.
Slowly lower the arm back to the starting position under control (2–3 seconds), keeping the elbow slightly bent and core braced.
Alternate and repeat on the other side, keeping the non-working arm relaxed by your side or lightly braced on your hip to maintain posture.
Keep breathing steadily (exhale while raising, inhale while lowering) and avoid using momentum or leaning the torso to lift heavier weight.
Adjust angle or lean slightly more forward if you feel the movement in the anterior delts more than the upper chest; reduce weight if the delts or traps take over.
exercises.description: A single-arm, standing diagonal raise that emphasizes the upper (clavicular) portion of the pectorals by moving the arm across the body and up toward the opposite shoulder. Performed alternately with each arm to isolate the upper chest while minimizing spinal movement.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and place two kettlebells on the floor at the head of the bench.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and place two kettlebells on the floor at the head of the bench.
Sit on the bench and brace your core, plant your feet flat on the floor, then lie back so your upper back and head are supported (supine on an incline bench).
Pick up the kettlebells with a secure grip and hold them at shoulder level with elbows below the wrists; palms can be neutral or slightly forward depending on comfort.
Retract your shoulder blades slightly and keep your chest lifted to create a stable pressing base.
Exhale and press the kettlebells up and slightly together until your arms are nearly extended but not locked out, keeping control and a roughly 45° elbow path from the torso.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and slowly lower the kettlebells back to shoulder level under control until they lightly touch or hover above your chest.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a braced core and avoiding excessive lower-back arch or flared elbows; lower with a controlled tempo to maximize muscle tension.
When finished, carefully return the kettlebells to the floor by sitting up and setting them down to avoid straining the shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: A pressing movement performed lying on an incline bench using kettlebells to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. The exercise also recruits the front delts and triceps while demanding grip stability from the forearms.

exercises.name: Close Grip Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and grasp it with a close, underhand (palms facing you / supinated) grip roughly shoulder-width or slightly narrower; thumbs can wrap around the bar.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and grasp it with a close, underhand (palms facing you / supinated) grip roughly shoulder-width or slightly narrower; thumbs can wrap around the bar.
Step or jump up so you are hanging with arms fully extended, legs either bent at the knees or crossed behind you to avoid touching the floor; maintain a slight hollow-body position with core engaged.
Begin the rep by depressing and slightly retracting the shoulder blades (set the scapula) to engage the lats before pulling with the arms.
Pull your body upward by driving the elbows down and back, leading with the chest and squeezing the lats and upper back until your chin clears the bar.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the torso stable and the biceps engaged.
Lower yourself slowly and under control back to a full dead hang with arms extended, maintaining tension in the lats and core throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding kipping or excessive swinging; breathe out while pulling up and inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A close-grip chin-up is an underhand (supinated) pull-up variation performed with a narrow grip that emphasizes the lats while placing significant demand on the biceps and forearms. The movement involves pulling the body up until the chin clears the bar, then lowering under control to a full hang.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Breeding
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your upper back and head are supported, plant your feet flat on the floor for stability (supine on incline bench).
Use your thighs to help bring the dumbbells up and hold them directly above your upper chest with a neutral or slight palms-facing position and a small bend in the elbows (start position).
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the shoulders.

Keeping the same slight elbow bend, lower the dumbbells in a wide arc out to your sides until you feel a stretch across the upper chest and anterior shoulder; do not let the elbows drop behind the plane of the bench.

Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the chest, then reverse the motion by squeezing the pectorals and bringing the dumbbells back up in the same arc until they meet over the upper chest.
Exhale as you press the weights together, inhale as you lower them, and perform each rep in a controlled manner to avoid using momentum.
Keep the neck relaxed, avoid shrugging the shoulders, and choose a weight that allows you to maintain form for the full set.
exercises.description: Incline dumbbell fly is a supine pressing/arc movement performed on an inclined bench that isolates the upper portion of the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc. It stretches the pectorals at the bottom and forces the chest to contract to bring the dumbbells together at the top.

exercises.name: Alternate Upper Chest Raise
exercises.notes: Start standing upright with feet hip-width apart, a slight soft bend in the knees and a 10–20° forward lean from the hips (not rounding the spine).
instructions: Start standing upright with feet hip-width apart, a slight soft bend in the knees and a 10–20° forward lean from the hips (not rounding the spine). Hold a light dumbbell or handle in one hand with the arm hanging at your side and the palm facing your body.
Brace your core and keep your chest lifted and shoulders down (no shrugging). Maintain a slight bend in the elbow throughout the movement.

Begin the rep by raising the working arm in a diagonal path across the front of your body and up toward the opposite shoulder/upper chest area (about 30–45° inward of straight front raise), rotating the palm slightly inward as you ascend.
Pause for 1 second at the top and actively squeeze the upper pectorals without letting the torso twist or the shoulder hike toward your ear.
Slowly lower the arm back to the starting position under control (2–3 seconds), keeping the elbow slightly bent and core braced.
Alternate and repeat on the other side, keeping the non-working arm relaxed by your side or lightly braced on your hip to maintain posture.
Keep breathing steadily (exhale while raising, inhale while lowering) and avoid using momentum or leaning the torso to lift heavier weight.
Adjust angle or lean slightly more forward if you feel the movement in the anterior delts more than the upper chest; reduce weight if the delts or traps take over.
exercises.description: A single-arm, standing diagonal raise that emphasizes the upper (clavicular) portion of the pectorals by moving the arm across the body and up toward the opposite shoulder. Performed alternately with each arm to isolate the upper chest while minimizing spinal movement.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.
