exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Chest Dip (on dip pull-up cage)
exercises.notes: Grip the parallel bars of the dip/pull-up cage with hands shoulder-width or slightly wider and arms fully extended; support your body weight with feet off the floor (knees can be bent or ankles crossed).
instructions: Grip the parallel bars of the dip/pull-up cage with hands shoulder-width or slightly wider and arms fully extended; support your body weight with feet off the floor (knees can be bent or ankles crossed).
Lean your torso forward ~20–30 degrees from vertical and retract your shoulder blades slightly to place emphasis on the pectorals; keep a neutral neck and core braced.
Inhale and slowly lower your body by bending the elbows and allowing them to flare mildly outward, keeping the forward lean so your chest moves toward the bars.
Descend until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor or your chest reaches the level of the bars (stop earlier if you feel shoulder pain); maintain control—do not drop suddenly.
Exhale and press through your palms to straighten the elbows and return to the start position with arms extended, keeping the forward lean and core engaged.
Perform reps with controlled tempo (e.g., 2–3 seconds down, 1 second pause, explosive up); avoid locking shoulders at the top and do not hyperextend the elbows.
If the movement is too difficult, use an assisted dip machine, resistance band support, or place feet on a low platform until you can perform unassisted reps with proper form.
exercises.description: A chest-focused dip performed on parallel bars/dip station with a forward lean to emphasize the pectorals while still engaging the triceps and delts. Keep legs off the floor and descend in a controlled arc until chest is level with the hands, then press back up.

exercises.name: Wide Grip Pull-Up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grasp the bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip, hands wider than shoulder-width.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grasp the bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip, hands wider than shoulder-width.
Start from a dead hang with arms fully extended, shoulders relaxed, legs either crossed at the ankles or slightly bent to reduce swinging; engage your core and slightly depress the shoulders.
Initiate the movement by retracting and depressing the shoulder blades (scapula) to engage the lats before bending the elbows.
Pull your body upward by driving your elbows down and back, keeping your chest aimed toward the bar and avoiding excessive forward head motion.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or your upper chest reaches the bar, squeezing the lats and upper back at the top for a brief pause.
Lower yourself under control back to the full dead hang by extending the elbows and allowing the shoulder blades to protract slightly, inhaling on the way down.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a controlled tempo and avoiding kipping or excessive swinging; breathe out during the pull and inhale during the descent.
exercises.description: A wide-grip pull-up is performed hanging from an overhead bar with a wide, pronated grip and pulling the torso upward by driving the elbows down and back to engage the lats and upper back. It emphasizes shoulder adduction and scapular retraction while the arms and grip assist.

exercises.name: Wide Grip Pull-Up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grasp it with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip wider than shoulder width.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grasp it with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip wider than shoulder width.
Jump or step up so you are hanging from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the floor (dead hang). Cross or bend your legs if necessary to avoid touching the ground.
Depress and slightly retract your shoulder blades to engage the back (avoid shrugging), and brace your core to prevent excessive swinging.
Exhale and pull your body straight up by driving your elbows down toward your hips, focusing on pulling with the lats and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Continue the pull until your chin clears the bar or your upper chest reaches the bar, keeping your torso as vertical as possible.
Inhale and lower yourself under control back to the dead hang with arms fully extended, keeping tension in the lats throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. If strict wide pull-ups are too difficult, use a band or assisted machine, or perform negatives (slow controlled lowers) until you build strength.
exercises.description: A vertical pulling exercise performed from a dead hang with a wide overhand grip that emphasizes the outer portion of the lats and upper back. It builds pulling strength and upper-body width while also engaging the arms and shoulder stabilizers.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Two Arm Military Press
exercises.notes: Step 1: Stand upright with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft, chest up and core braced.
instructions: Step 1: Stand upright with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft, chest up and core braced.

Step 2: Clean the kettlebells to a rack position at shoulder height with the kettlebell bodies resting against the forearms and handles across the palms; elbows should be slightly in front of the torso.
Step 3: Grip the handles firmly to engage the forearms and maintain a neutral wrist (avoid excessive wrist bend).
Step 4: Inhale and brace your abs and glutes to prevent lower back extension; keep head neutral and eyes forward.

Step 5: Exhale and press both kettlebells straight up in a controlled path until the arms are fully extended overhead and the shoulders are locked out; push your elbows slightly together at the top and avoid shrugging excessively.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the kettlebells back to the rack position under control while inhaling.
Step 7: Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a tight core and upright torso on every repetition.
Step 8: Common form cues — do not hyperextend the spine (keep ribs down), avoid flaring elbows out too wide, and keep movements smooth to reduce momentum.
exercises.description: A standing two‑handed kettlebell military press performed from the rack position to drive the weights overhead, primarily working the shoulder delts. The movement also requires triceps extension, upper‑back stabilization and core/bracing to maintain an upright posture.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward (or slightly rotated) and elbows bent roughly 90 degrees, wrists stacked over elbows.
Inhale, then exhale as you press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until your arms are nearly extended overhead; avoid fully locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the shoulders down and chest lifted (do not shrug excessively).
Inhale as you slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Keep the back pressed against the bench and avoid excessive lower-back arching or leaning; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press performed from a back-supported bench to drive the weights overhead, primarily working the shoulder (deltoid) muscles with triceps and upper traps assisting. The movement emphasizes vertical pressing strength and shoulder stability.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Two Arm Clean
exercises.notes: Set your shoulders back, engage your core, and pull the kettlebells slightly back between your legs (loaded hinge) to create tension—this is the starting swing.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, kettlebells on the floor between your feet and handles facing you; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine to grip both handles.
Set your shoulders back, engage your core, and pull the kettlebells slightly back between your legs (loaded hinge) to create tension—this is the starting swing.
Drive forcefully through the hips by extending the hips and knees explosively (hip snap) while keeping the arms relaxed so the momentum of the bells rises.

As the kettlebells rise, allow your elbows to bend and the hands to rotate so the kettlebells flip up and land softly into the front-rack position on the forearms and biceps (wrists stacked under elbows); avoid pulling primarily with the arms.
Catch the bells close to your body with elbows tucked and palms facing inward or slightly toward your chest; absorb the load with the hips and legs, keeping the torso upright and core braced.
Reset by slightly hinging the hips to guide the kettlebells down, reversing the wrist rotation so the bells pass close between the legs and return to the starting swing position.

Repeat for the prescribed reps, maintaining a neutral spine, controlled breathing, and using hip drive rather than excessive arm pull; if fatigue causes rounding of the back or sloppy rack position, reduce weight.
exercises.description: A standing, compound movement that uses an explosive hip drive to bring two kettlebells from between the legs to the front-rack position; it develops the posterior chain and upper-body stability for the catch. Perform controlled swings and a short pull so the bells rotate smoothly into the rack on the forearms.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with your arms fully extended; keep your head aligned with your spine.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows back and up, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs or hip area while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, maintaining tightness through the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with controlled motion until your arms are extended.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, keeping the torso fixed (avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation) and continue to breathe: exhale while pulling, inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A bent-over row performed with dumbbells where you hinge at the hips and pull the weights toward your torso, emphasizing the lats and upper back. The movement also engages the arms and posterior chain to stabilize the hinged position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to an incline of ~30-45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad and feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to an incline of ~30-45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad and feet planted on the floor.

Pick up a pair of dumbbells and hold them at shoulder height with a neutral to slightly forward grip (palms facing forward or slightly rotated), elbows roughly below or slightly in front of the shoulders.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades gently down and back to stabilize the upper back, and keep a slight natural arch in the spine without overarching.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly extended overhead (do not lock the elbows aggressively).
Pause briefly at the top with the weights balanced, keeping the shoulders and core engaged.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position, maintaining control and keeping the elbows from flaring excessively.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining steady tempo and avoiding momentum or excessive lower-back drive.
exercises.description: Seated incline dumbbell shoulder press performed on an inclined bench to emphasize the anterior delts while also recruiting the triceps and upper chest. Press the dumbbells overhead from shoulder height and lower with control.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Feet Flat Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
instructions: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
Lie supine on the flat bench with your eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and planted for stability, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades (pinch them together and press them into the bench) to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the bar with a full grip slightly wider than shoulder width, wrists stacked over forearms and elbows under the bar.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar into the start position directly above your shoulders.
Inhale, then lower the bar in a controlled manner to mid-chest / nipple line, keeping elbows at about a 45° angle to the torso and maintaining scapular retraction.
Briefly pause with the bar lightly contacting the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar upward along a slightly arced path back to full arm extension.
Lock out the elbows at the top without overextending, keep the feet planted and core braced throughout the set.
After the final rep, carefully rack the bar onto the supports or have your spotter assist; use a spotter for heavy loads and maintain strict control on every rep.
exercises.description: Barbell feet-flat flat bench press is a supine pressing exercise that primarily works the pectorals by pressing a loaded barbell from the chest to full arm extension. Keeping the feet flat provides a stable base while the triceps and delts assist in elbow extension and shoulder stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
instructions: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
Lie supine on the incline bench with your head supported, feet flat on the floor, and back slightly arched while maintaining scapular retraction.
Grip the barbell with a pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your upper chest/shoulder line, then stabilize before starting the descent.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the upper chest/clavicular area, keeping elbows at roughly a 30–60° angle from the torso (avoid flaring to 90°).
Lightly touch or stop just above the chest, pause briefly without bouncing, then exhale and press the bar upward and slightly back to the start position, fully extending the elbows.
Keep core braced and feet planted throughout to maintain stability and prevent excessive lower back extension.
Carefully re-rack the bar under control or have a spotter assist when finished.
exercises.description: A barbell press performed on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest by pressing the bar upward and slightly toward the clavicle. It also engages the front delts and triceps for pressing and locking out the weight.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Landmine Shoulders Press with Viking Press Attachment
exercises.notes: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; grip the Viking press handles so the attachment sits at shoulder height with elbows roughly tucked under the wrists.
instructions: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; grip the Viking press handles so the attachment sits at shoulder height with elbows roughly tucked under the wrists.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neutral spine, and position the bar angled away from you so it can travel upward without hitting your head.
Inhale, then press the handles upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your arms are fully extended overhead or slightly behind the head, avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged and elbows unlocked but not hyperextended.
Exhale as you slowly lower the handles back down to the starting shoulder-height position under control, maintaining tension in the delts and core.
Repeat for the desired reps; keep movement smooth and controlled, avoid leaning the torso excessively back or using lower-body drive to move the load.
Adjust stance or hand position to shift emphasis (wider grip = more lateral delt emphasis; closer grip = more anterior delt/pectorals) and lower the weight if you cannot maintain strict form.
exercises.description: A standing landmine press using a Viking press attachment that emphasizes overhead pressing from the shoulders; the movement drives the handles upward from shoulder height to full arm extension, targeting the delts while the triceps and upper chest assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Palm-in Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with feet flat on the floor.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand and sit upright on the bench, then lie back so your head, upper back, and glutes are supported (supine on an incline).
Hold the dumbbells at shoulder level with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and elbows slightly tucked (~30–45 degrees from the torso).
Brace your core, retract your shoulder blades, and keep your feet planted.
Exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly toward each other until your arms are nearly extended but not fully locked.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position with controlled tension on the pectorals.
Keep wrists neutral (don’t let them bend), maintain the incline bench contact, and avoid flaring the elbows straight out to minimize shoulder strain.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions with steady, controlled tempo.
exercises.description: An incline dumbbell press performed with a neutral (palms-in) grip that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest while reducing stress on the shoulders. Performed on an incline bench, pressing the weights up and slightly together from chest level.

exercises.name: Barbell Pause Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and load the bar with appropriate weight; position safety pins or have a spotter if available.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and load the bar with appropriate weight; position safety pins or have a spotter if available.
Lie supine on the incline bench with your head near the top, feet flat on the floor, knees bent and planted for stability.
Retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together to create a stable upper-back base; maintain a slight natural arch in your lower back.
Grip the bar with a medium (shoulder-width to slightly wider) grip, wrists stacked over elbows and elbows pointed roughly 45 degrees from your torso.
Unrack the bar and hold it straight above your shoulders with arms extended; take a full breath in and brace your core.
Lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (not the throat), keeping the bar path slightly diagonal toward the upper chest.
Pause and hold the bar motionless on your chest for 1-2 seconds without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the scapulae retracted.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively but under control until your arms are near full extension, avoiding locking out aggressively.
Repeat for the desired reps, then carefully rack the bar back on the uprights with control.
exercises.description: A barbell incline bench press with a deliberate pause at the bottom to increase time under tension and focus on the upper chest. The movement emphasizes controlled lowering to the upper chest/clavicular area, a short isometric pause, then a powerful press to near-full extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at your thighs.

Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back. Retract your shoulder blades and keep them contacting the bench.
Start with the dumbbells at mid-chest, elbows bent about 90 degrees and wrists neutral (dumbbell handles aligned with forearms).
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly straight but not hyperextended; keep wrists stacked over elbows.

Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position, maintaining control and stopping when the dumbbells reach chest level or your elbows form ~90 degrees. Keep elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from your torso to protect the shoulders.
Keep your core braced and feet firmly planted for stability throughout each rep; avoid excessive flaring of the ribs or letting the shoulders lift off the bench.
Use a spotter for heavy loads and choose a weight that allows controlled full-range reps with good form.
exercises.description: Flat dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a bench, pressing two dumbbells upward from chest level to develop the pectorals; the movement also recruits the anterior delts and triceps for stability and lockout.

exercises.name: Lever Crossovers
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so the handles are at mid-chest level when you sit upright. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are at mid-chest level when you sit upright.
Sit with your back pressed firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees at about 90°.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-down grip and start with your arms opened out to the sides at shoulder height, elbows slightly bent.
Brace your core and keep your torso stable; avoid leaning forward or arching the lower back.
Exhale and bring the handles together in a slow, controlled arc in front of your chest, allowing the hands to cross slightly if the machine path allows.
Pause briefly at full contraction and focus on squeezing the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position, maintaining the slight bend in the elbows and control throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping tension on the chest and avoiding momentum.
exercises.description: Seated lever crossovers are a machine chest fly variation that brings the arms together in front of the body to isolate and load the pectorals while minimizing torso movement. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and a controlled arc to squeeze the chest at peak contraction.

exercises.name: Lever Bent Over Neutral Grip Row (with chest support)
exercises.notes: Adjust the chest pad and lever so your torso is supported comfortably on the pad with the handles within reach; select an appropriate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the chest pad and lever so your torso is supported comfortably on the pad with the handles within reach; select an appropriate weight.
Stand with your chest pressed to the support (prone chest-supported position), feet shoulder-width or slightly staggered on the floor, knees slightly bent and spine neutral.
Reach down and grasp the handles with a neutral grip (palms facing each other); allow the arms to extend fully while maintaining tension in the shoulder blades.
Initiate the rep by retracting your shoulder blades and pulling the handles toward your lower chest/ribcage, keeping elbows tucked close to the body and moving in a controlled arc.
Squeeze the lats and mid/upper back at the top of the movement for a brief pause, avoiding excessive shrugging or leaning off the pad.
Slowly extend your arms and allow the shoulders to protract under control until you feel a stretch in the lats, keeping the torso supported on the chest pad throughout.
Breathe out while pulling (concentric) and inhale while returning (eccentric); repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.description: A chest-supported neutral-grip lever row performed on a lever machine that isolates the mid and lower lats while minimizing lower-back involvement. The chest pad supports the torso so you pull the handles toward the ribcage with a neutral (palms-facing) grip.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at your thighs.

Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back. Retract your shoulder blades and keep them contacting the bench.
Start with the dumbbells at mid-chest, elbows bent about 90 degrees and wrists neutral (dumbbell handles aligned with forearms).
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly straight but not hyperextended; keep wrists stacked over elbows.

Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position, maintaining control and stopping when the dumbbells reach chest level or your elbows form ~90 degrees. Keep elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from your torso to protect the shoulders.
Keep your core braced and feet firmly planted for stability throughout each rep; avoid excessive flaring of the ribs or letting the shoulders lift off the bench.
Use a spotter for heavy loads and choose a weight that allows controlled full-range reps with good form.
exercises.description: Flat dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a bench, pressing two dumbbells upward from chest level to develop the pectorals; the movement also recruits the anterior delts and triceps for stability and lockout.

exercises.name: Lever Front Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine weight and set the seat height so your feet are flat on the floor and the thigh pads rest snugly on your thighs to prevent lifting. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the machine weight and set the seat height so your feet are flat on the floor and the thigh pads rest snugly on your thighs to prevent lifting.
Sit upright on the seat (seated), reach up and grasp the front/wide handles with an overhand (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder width; arms should start almost fully extended.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back (scapular depression/retraction) before initiating the pull to engage the lats.
Exhale and pull the handles straight down toward the upper chest/front, leading with your elbows and keeping your torso mostly upright (allow a very slight lean back but avoid excessive swinging).
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the bottom of the movement, keeping the wrists neutral and elbows pointing down/back, then pause briefly.
Inhale and slowly control the weight as you extend your arms back up to the start position, allowing the shoulder blades to fully stretch without shrugging the shoulders.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping motion smooth and avoiding momentum or pulling with the neck; lower weight if you cannot maintain full scapular control.
exercises.description: Seated lever front pulldown using a wide overhead handle to pull resistance down in front of the chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus region. Performed seated with thigh pads to stabilize the body while pulling the handles to the upper chest.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Cable Wide Grip Lat Pulldown

instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.

Sit tall with a slight natural arch in the lower back, feet flat, chest up and head neutral.
Begin the rep by inhaling, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back toward your ribcage.
Lead the motion with your elbows and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom, bringing the bar to the upper chest or collarbone level.
Pause briefly while maintaining tension in the lats, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive backward lean or swinging.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with controlled extension of the arms until you feel a full stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and controlled breathing throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip lat pulldown using an overhand wide bar to pull the cable down toward the upper chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus dorsi. Performed seated with knees braced to isolate the back and minimize leg drive.

Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.

exercises.name: Lever Incline Single Arm Chest Press

instructions: Adjust the incline seat and lever so the handle starts level with the mid to upper chest; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your back pressed into the bench, feet flat on the floor, and the non-working hand resting on your thigh or the seat for balance.
Grasp the handle with one hand; slightly retract the shoulder blade on the working side and brace your abs to prevent torso rotation.
Exhale and press the handle upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until the arm is nearly extended (do not hyperextend the elbow).
Inhale and slowly lower the handle back to the starting chest level under control, maintaining scapular stability and an upright torso.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping hips square and avoiding twisting; switch arms and perform the same number of reps on the opposite side.
exercises.description: Seated single-arm press on an incline lever machine that emphasizes the upper chest (pectoral) on the working side while requiring shoulder and triceps assistance and core stabilization. Performed one arm at a time to isolate each side and reduce compensation.

exercises.name: Cable seated row
exercises.notes: Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent (seated, upright position).
Grab the handle (V-bar or close grip) with a neutral or overhand grip and extend your arms so the weight stack is lifted and shoulders are protracted; maintain a neutral spine and chest slightly up.
Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
Keep the torso stable—avoid excessive leaning or using momentum; maintain a slight forward trunk angle only if required to reach the handle, then return to a tall, braced posture as you pull.

Squeeze the shoulder blades and lats at the end range for a brief 1–2 second pause, then slowly extend the arms forward under control to the start position while allowing the scapulae to move forward.
Breathe out while pulling and inhale while returning to the start; choose a weight that allows controlled form and full elbow extension between reps.
exercises.description: Seated cable row is a horizontal pulling exercise performed sitting with feet braced that primarily targets the lats while working the middle/upper back and arm pulling muscles. It emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive to pull the handle to the torso.

Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Set a single D-handle or two low pulleys to the lowest position and select an appropriate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set a single D-handle or two low pulleys to the lowest position and select an appropriate weight.
Sit on a bench in front of the low pulley(s) with feet flat, then hinge forward at the hips so your torso is leaning over your thighs (seated, bent-over position).
Reach down and grasp the handle(s) with a neutral or palms-facing position; keep a slight bend in the elbows and let the arms hang toward the floor.
Keeping your chest slightly lifted and core braced, externally rotate the shoulders and raise your upper arms out to the sides in a wide arc until they are roughly parallel to the floor.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the rear delts/upper back, avoiding shoulder shrugging or using momentum.
Slowly lower the handles back to the starting position under control and repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a steady tempo, avoid swinging the torso, and use a lighter weight if you cannot keep the elbows slightly bent and the movement strict.
exercises.description: Seated cable rear delt raise is a bent-over, seated lateral raise performed with a low cable to isolate the posterior (rear) deltoid. It emphasizes controlled horizontal abduction of the shoulder while minimizing torso movement.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Set a single D-handle or two low pulleys to the lowest position and select an appropriate weight. Train to muscular failure.

Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the rear delts/upper back, avoiding shoulder shrugging or using momentum.

exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.

exercises.name: Lever Incline Chest Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so that the handles are aligned with the upper chest/ clavicle area when seated and set an appropriate weight on the lever. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat so that the handles are aligned with the upper chest/ clavicle area when seated and set an appropriate weight on the lever.
Sit with your back and head pressed firmly against the incline pad, feet flat on the floor or platform for stability.
Reach up and grasp the handles with a neutral or slightly overhand grip, elbows bent and positioned slightly below shoulder height at the start.
Brace your core and exhale as you press the handles upward and slightly together until your arms are nearly extended; avoid forcefully locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top without shrugging shoulders or arching the lower back.
Inhale and slowly control the handles back down to the starting position with elbows tracking under the wrists and chest muscles lengthening.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining full contact between your back/head and the pad and keeping movement smooth and controlled.
If you feel excessive shoulder strain, reduce range of motion or weight and ensure the seat/handle alignment is correct.
exercises.description: Seated incline lever chest press performed on an incline pressing machine that targets the upper portion of the chest by pressing the handles upward and slightly together from chest level. The back remains supported on the inclined pad while the shoulders and triceps assist the movement.

exercises.name: Lever Chest Press (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Load plates evenly on both pegs and check that the lever is securely seated. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Load plates evenly on both pegs and check that the lever is securely seated.
Sit upright on the machine with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor; adjust the seat so the handles are at mid-chest level.
Grasp the handles with a comfortable grip (palms facing forward or neutral depending on the handle) and tuck your shoulder blades slightly together to stabilize the chest.
Start with elbows bent roughly 80–100 degrees and forearms roughly perpendicular to the floor.
Exhale and press the handles forward and slightly together in a controlled arc until your arms are almost fully extended; do not lock your elbows at the top.
Pause briefly at full extension while maintaining a tight chest and stable trunk.
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position under control until your elbows return near chest level.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping wrists neutral, spine supported by the pad, and avoiding jerky movements or hyperextension of the shoulders.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever chest press that targets the pectorals by pressing fixed lever arms forward from the chest. The movement also engages the delts and triceps for shoulder and elbow extension.

exercises.name: Lever T-bar Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Stand on the machine foot platforms with feet hip-width apart and straddle the bar, grasping the handles with a neutral or overhand grip. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand on the machine foot platforms with feet hip-width apart and straddle the bar, grasping the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is inclined forward (maintain a neutral spine and avoid rounding), and brace your core.
Begin with your arms extended and shoulders protracted so the weight is under control.
Exhale and pull the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining scapular retraction and keeping the torso stable (avoid excessive torso lean or momentum).
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start under control until arms are nearly extended, keeping tension in the back throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements smooth and avoiding shrugging the shoulders or rounding the lower back.
Adjust foot position or torso angle as needed to maintain a comfortable, neutral spine and full range of motion.
exercises.description: A bent-over plate-loaded T-bar row where you stand on the foot platform and hinge at the hips to pull the handle toward your torso, primarily working the lats and mid-back. The movement emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive while maintaining a neutral spine.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your head, upper back and glutes are supported (supine on an incline bench) and place your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help rack the dumbbells and press them overhead so your arms are extended above your chest, palms facing each other (neutral grip); keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract your shoulder blades slightly and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement; avoid excessive lower-back arching.

Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells in a wide, controlled arc out to your sides until you feel a stretch in the upper chest—stop when your elbows are roughly in line with the bench or slightly below shoulder level.
Keep the elbows fixed at the same slight bend throughout the descent; do not let the elbows drop or the wrists bend excessively.
Exhale and bring the dumbbells back together over your chest by squeezing the pectorals, following the same arc path used on the way down.
Pause briefly at the top with the weights close together (but avoid slamming them), then repeat for the desired number of reps.
Use a lighter weight if you feel shoulder joint pain; focus on controlled range of motion and chest contraction rather than heavy load.
exercises.description: Lying on an incline bench, perform a wide-arc dumbbell fly to emphasize the upper portion of the chest; movement stretches the chest as you lower and contracts the pectorals as you bring the weights back together. Keep a slight bend in the elbows and move in a controlled arc to avoid shoulder strain.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell by each knee. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell by each knee.
Lie back so your head, upper back and glutes are supported (supine on incline) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Pick up the dumbbells and bring them to your sides (low position) with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows; retract the shoulder blades slightly to protect the shoulders.
Initiate the movement by squeezing the pectorals and lifting the dumbbells in a wide arc toward each other above the upper chest, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout.
Pause briefly at the top with the dumbbells close but not forcefully smashed together; exhale during the concentric (lifting) phase.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back down along the same arc to the low starting position under control, inhaling during the descent and maintaining tension in the chest.
Keep the torso stable (avoid excessive lumbar arching or using leg drive) and limit range if you feel shoulder pain; perform all reps with controlled tempo.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while focusing on chest contraction rather than moving the weight with momentum.
exercises.description: Lying on an incline bench, perform a dumbbell fly from a low starting position to emphasize the upper chest (pectorals) by arcing the arms upward and together. Keep a small bend in the elbows and control the descent to maintain tension on the chest.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Single Arm Neutral Grip Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing position). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in one hand at your side with a neutral grip (thumb pointing forward/up) and a slight bend in the elbow.
Keep your torso still and shoulders down and back; avoid leaning or swinging.
Exhale and raise the dumbbell straight in front of you with a controlled motion until your arm reaches about shoulder height (parallel to the floor).
Keep the wrist neutral and maintain the slight elbow bend throughout the movement.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and slowly lower the weight back to the starting position under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch arms. Reduce weight if you cannot maintain a rigid torso or if you swing to lift the weight.
exercises.description: Standing single-arm front raise using a neutral (thumbs-up) grip to isolate the anterior deltoid; performed with a slight elbow bend and controlled lift to shoulder height. The core stabilizes the torso while the forearm maintains the grip.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Palm Rotational Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is around 30–45 degrees from vertical (leaning forward), keeping the spine neutral and eyes looking a few feet ahead; allow the arms to hang straight down (palms can start facing each other/neutral). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand; knees slightly bent.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is around 30–45 degrees from vertical (leaning forward), keeping the spine neutral and eyes looking a few feet ahead; allow the arms to hang straight down (palms can start facing each other/neutral).
Brace your core and depress your shoulder blades (avoid shrugging) to stabilize the spine.

Initiate the row by driving your elbows back and upwards, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs; as you pull, rotate your wrists so the palms turn (supinate) toward your body/ceiling at the top of the movement.
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the top for a brief pause, keeping elbows close to the body and chest steady (no torso rotation).
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position while rotating the palms back to the starting orientation; maintain the hip hinge and neutral spine.
Repeat for the desired reps, exhaling as you pull and inhaling as you lower. Maintain controlled tempo and avoid rounding the lower back.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell row performed from a hip-hinge position where the wrists rotate during the pull to emphasize the lats and upper back while also engaging the arms and rear delts. The movement combines a horizontal row with a palm rotation to increase range of motion and elbow extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Palm-in Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with feet flat on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with feet flat on the floor.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand and sit upright on the bench, then lie back so your head, upper back, and glutes are supported (supine on an incline).
Hold the dumbbells at shoulder level with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and elbows slightly tucked (~30–45 degrees from the torso).
Brace your core, retract your shoulder blades, and keep your feet planted.
Exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly toward each other until your arms are nearly extended but not fully locked.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position with controlled tension on the pectorals.
Keep wrists neutral (don’t let them bend), maintain the incline bench contact, and avoid flaring the elbows straight out to minimize shoulder strain.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions with steady, controlled tempo.
exercises.description: An incline dumbbell press performed with a neutral (palms-in) grip that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest while reducing stress on the shoulders. Performed on an incline bench, pressing the weights up and slightly together from chest level.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
